items.question: If I ___ Japanese, I'd apply for the job now.
explanations: Correct! An unreal present takes the second conditional: 'If I spoke Japanese'.
'Had spoken' is the third conditional, about the past.
'Speak' (present) is for real situations; this is imaginary.
The if-clause never uses 'would'.

items.question: If I ___ Japanese, I would have got that job last year.
explanations: 'Spoke' (past simple) is the second conditional; 'last year' needs the past perfect.
Correct! An unreal past takes the third conditional: 'If I had spoken Japanese'.
'Speak' is present simple, but this is about the past.
The if-clause never uses 'would'.

items.question: If she had left earlier, she ___ the train.
explanations: 'Would catch' is the second conditional; a past condition needs 'would have'.
Correct! An unreal past result uses 'would have caught'.
'Catches' is present simple, but this is about the past.
'Caught' (past simple) is a plain fact, not the imagined result.

items.question: If I had a bigger flat, I ___ a dog.
explanations: Correct! An unreal present takes the second conditional: 'I would get a dog'.
'Would have got' is the third conditional, about the past.
'Will get' is a real future, not this imaginary situation.
'Got' (past simple) doesn't express the imagined result.

items.question: Which sentence is about the PAST?
options: If I were taller, I'd play basketball.
If I had trained harder, I would have won.
If I had a car, I'd drive to work.
If I spoke French, I'd move to Paris.
explanations: This is the second conditional — an unreal present.
Correct! The third conditional imagines the past: 'If I had trained harder, I would have won'.
This is the second conditional — an unreal present.
This is the second conditional — an unreal present/future.

items.question: If I ___ about the meeting, I would have come yesterday.
explanations: 'Knew' (past simple) is the second conditional; 'yesterday' needs the past perfect.
Correct! An unreal past takes the third conditional: 'If I had known'.
'Know' is present simple, but this is about the past.
The if-clause never uses 'would'.

items.question: If we ___ more money, we'd buy a house these days.
explanations: Correct! 'These days' is present, so use the second conditional: 'If we had more money'.
'Had had' is the third conditional, about the past.
'Have' (present) is for real situations; this is imaginary.
The if-clause never uses 'would'.

items.question: Correct this: 'If I knew, I would have told you.'
options: If I had known, I would have told you.
If I knew, I would tell you.
If I know, I will tell you.
explanations: Correct! A finished past needs the third conditional: 'If I had known, I would have told you'.
'If I knew, I would tell you' is a valid second conditional, but changes the meaning to now.
It mixes a second-conditional if-clause with a third-conditional result.
'If I know, I'll tell you' is a first conditional (real future).

items.question: If he weren't so rude, people ___ him more. (in general, now)
explanations: Correct! A general present takes the second conditional: 'people would like him more'.
'Would have liked' is the third conditional, about the past.
'Will like' is a real future, not this imaginary situation.
'Liked' (past simple) doesn't express the imagined result.

items.question: Rewrite about the past: 'If I were rich, I'd help.' →
options: If I had been rich, I would have helped.
If I was rich, I'd have helped.
If I am rich, I will help.
If I had been rich, I would help.
explanations: Correct! The past version is the third conditional: 'If I had been rich, I would have helped'.
'If I was rich' isn't the past perfect needed for the third conditional.
'If I am rich, I'll help' is a first conditional (real future).
This mixes a past-perfect condition with a present result.
